title: Interpolation and Density Estimation: Tasks
text:
Data
The airqualtiy dataset (luftqalitaet.gpkg) contains measurements of nitrogen dioxide NO2 from 2015 for 97 monitoring sites in Switzerland. Nitrogen dioxide is produced when fuels and combustibles are burned, especially at high combustion temperatures, with road traffic being the main source. You can find more information on this here

title: Interpolation and Density Estimation: Tasks
text:
Reading in Data and Explore

library(sf)
library(terra)
library(tmap)
library(gstat)
library(tidyverse)
library(spatstat)

In order to read in I am using sf geopakage parser. For exercies 1 I also need a polygon that delimits the swiss national teretorium. This can be gotten from the swissBOUNDARIES3D dataset. So I am reading this in too. For some reason I also have to correctly set the swiss CRS for the swissBOUNDARIES3D data. This is luckely provided in the air quality data, so I do not have to look up any EPSG codes.

air_quality <- sf::st_read("data/luftqualitaet.gpkg", quiet = TRUE)

sf::st_crs(air_quality) <- 2056

swiss_polygon <- sf::st_read("data/swissBOUNDARIES3D_1_5_LV95_LN02.gdb", layer = "TLM_LANDESGEBIET", quiet = TRUE) %>%
st_transform(crs = st_crs(air_quality))

Lets Inspect the data. For this I am using tmap, as it allows for an easy little Dashboard to explore the data

tmap_mode("view")

ℹ tmap modes "plot" - "view"
ℹ toggle with `tmap::ttm()`

tm_shape(air_quality) +
    tm_polygons()





As expected, a bunch of points with values.

title: Interpolation and Density Estimation: Tasks
text:
Solution 1: IDW

First, a grid needs to be created with sf::st_make_grid. The cellsize argument defines the edge length of each square grid cell in map units (here meters). So cellsize = 5000 produces a grid of 5000 m × 5000 m squares, and what = "centers" returns the centroid of each cell as the prediction location. Smaller cell sizes yield a finer resolution but are heavier to compute. I am using sf::st_filter to filter out points which do not lie in Switzerland. This is necessary because sf::st_make_grid simply produces a rectangular grid, which would then also include regions of the neighbouring countries.

grid <- sf::st_make_grid(
swiss_polygon ,
        cellsize = 5000,
        what = "centers",
        ) %>%
    sf::st_as_sf(
        crs = sf::st_crs(air_quality)
    ) %>%
    sf::st_filter(swiss_polygon, .predicate = st_within)

Lets have a look on how this grid looks like:

tmap_mode("plot")

ℹ tmap modes "plot" - "view"

tm_shape(grid) +
    tm_dots() +
    tm_title("Prediction Grid (5 km × 5 km, centers)")








Looks decent. All the interpolation points lie within Switzerland. Then lets go ahead and perform nearest neighbour Interpolation.

idw_result <- gstat::idw(
    formula = value ~ 1,
    locations = air_quality,
    newdata = grid,
    idp = 2
)

[inverse distance weighted interpolation]


Concerting the interpolated grid to a raster can be done using terra. For this I am first creating a Dataframe where each row represents a interpolated point. The dataframe holds three columns. The third column holds the interpolated NO_2 concentrations the first and second column hold the coordinates of the point.

preds <- idw_result %>%
    sf::st_drop_geometry() %>%
    select(var1.pred) %>%
    as_tibble()

prediction_df <- idw_result %>%
    st_coordinates() %>%
    as_tibble() %>%
    mutate(
        interpolated_values = preds
    ) %>% 
        unpack(interpolated_values)

prediction_df %>% head()

# A tibble: 6 × 3
         X        Y var1.pred
     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
1 2722910. 1077768.      38.0
2 2572910. 1082768.      22.0
3 2577910. 1082768.      22.1
4 2582910. 1082768.      22.3
5 2717910. 1082768.      42.9
6 2722910. 1082768.      40.0


Then terra::rast can be used to create a raster from the dataframe using type="xyz". This tells terra that the first two columns will the coordinates, and the third column holds a point attribute.

interpolation_raster <- prediction_df %>% 
    terra::rast(type="xyz")


tmap_mode("plot")

ℹ tmap modes "plot" - "view"

tm_shape(interpolation_raster) +
  tm_raster(
    col = "var1.pred",
    col.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
    col.legend = tm_legend(title = "NO₂ (µg/m³)")
  ) +
  tm_title("IDW Interpolation of NO₂ Concentrations (idp=2, nmax=Inf)")









Parameter Exploration
The exercise asks to vary idp, nmax / nmix, and maxdist. I did that. Below I will discus the effect of the parameters for a specific case where I vary idp and nmax. The low vacet plots shows the result of nearest neighbour interpolations for the parameters idp ∈ {1, 2, 5} and nmax ∈ {10, 30, Inf} (where Inf means all stations contribute). Each result is rasterised and displayed in a 3×3 facet grid.

idp_values  <- c(1, 2, 5)
nmax_values <- c(10, 30, Inf)

param_grid <- expand.grid(idp = idp_values, nmax = nmax_values)

idw_to_raster <- function(idp, nmax, locations, newdata) {
  nmax_arg <- if (is.infinite(nmax)) Inf else nmax
  result <- gstat::idw(
    formula  = value ~ 1,
    locations = locations,
    newdata   = newdata,
    idp       = idp,
    nmax      = nmax_arg
  )
  coords <- st_coordinates(result)
  vals   <- result$var1.pred
  df     <- data.frame(X = coords[,1], Y = coords[,2], value = vals)
  terra::rast(df, type = "xyz", crs = st_crs(air_quality)$wkt)
}

raster_list <- purrr::pmap(param_grid, function(idp, nmax) {
  idw_to_raster(idp, nmax, air_quality, grid)
})

[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]

tmap_mode("plot")

map_list <- purrr::pmap(list(raster_list, param_grid$idp, param_grid$nmax), function(r, idp, nmax) {
  nmax_label <- if (is.infinite(nmax)) "all" else as.character(nmax)
  tm_shape(r) +
    tm_raster(
      col = "value",
      col.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
      col.legend = tm_legend(show = FALSE)
    ) +
    tm_title(text = paste0("idp=", idp, ", nmax=", nmax_label))
})

tmap_arrange(maps = map_list, ncol = 3, nrow = 3)










Interpretation
When reading the visualisation row-whise, one can deduce the effect of different inverse distance weighting powers (idp) on the calculation. The pattern is more plurred out for low idp values and becomes more and more varied and distinct for higher idp values. This, because for large idp values a specific measuring point also contributes meaningfully to further distanced interpolation points. So two neighbouring interpolated points are rather similar in nature, as they get influenced by the same large group of neighbouring points. As idp increases however, the influence of a measurement point on a interpolated point drops much quicker with distance. As an effect, two neighbouring interpolated points suddently get influenced by mostly different measured points. They therefore are also very different in nature as a results the whole graphic is less “smeared out”.
When reading the visualisaiton column-whise on can study theh influence of nmax. The first row provides a great example. For larger values of nmax the differences in NO_2 concentration get blurrier. This is because for larger nmax, each minterpolation point gets influenced by more and more measuring points. Ergo two neighbouring interpolation points become similar and similar, as the set of measurement points that influence both interpolation points gets larger, hence the neighbouring points become more similar.
The lower left and the upper right conner show extreme cases. Here the homogenizing effect of having a slow weighting decay and the maximal number of measuring points involved in the calculation, yields a smeared out / blurry results. In the upper right, having a fast decay and a small number of points for calculation yields very sharp boundaries.


Exercise 2: Nearest Neighbour
Another simple option for interpolation is the nearest neighbour approach, that we can recreate using voronoi polygons. Use the approach described in Exercise 2: Voronoi to create voronoi polygons. Turn the resulting sfc object to sf using st_as_sf, then use st_join to add the measured NO2 values to the polygons.
Visualize the result

title: Interpolation and Density Estimation: Tasks
text:
### Solution 2

voronoi <- air_quality %>%
    sf::st_geometry() %>%               # extract the point geometries (sfc)
    sf::st_union() %>%                  # collapse into one MULTIPOINT geometry collection
    sf::st_voronoi() %>%                # compute the Voronoi tessellation (returns GEOMETRYCOLLECTION)
    sf::st_collection_extract("POLYGON") %>%  # unpack individual polygons
    sf::st_as_sf() %>%                  # wrap in an sf data frame
    sf::st_intersection(swiss_polygon) %>%    # clip to Switzerland
    sf::st_join(air_quality)            # attach the NO2 values from the nearest station

tm_shape(voronoi) +
    tm_polygons(
      fill = "value",
      fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
      fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "NO₂ (µg/m³)")
    ) +
    tm_title("Nearest Neighbour NO₂ Interpolation (Voronoi)") +
    tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE, legend.outside.position = "right")








This example shows some shortcomings of Voronoi Polygons quite neatly.

The interpolation is not continuous (as you get into higher mathematics, for some reason everyone gets obsessed with having continuous smooth phenomena, not shure wheter this is just me). Technically not realy that much of a problem, but for NO2 concentration it is just unrealistic. The concentration of a gas in the air is for shure continuous and not a discretely changing phenomenon.
Large regions have the exact same value of NO2. This is of course also not realy realistic, since for a gas we can assume that its concentration decreases with the distance of emmitance. Hence, having patches with exactly the same value does not correctly model, how gas concentrations realy behave in the atmosphere.

title: Interpolation and Density Estimation: Tasks
text:
Density Estimation

Data
The data set rotmilan.gpkg originates from a larger research project of the Sempach Ornithological Institute which can be accessed via the movebank platform (see Scherler 2020). This is a single individual that has been fitted with a transmitter since 2017 and is travelling across the whole of Central Europe. In this exercise, we only work with the data points that were recorded in Switzerland. If you would like to analyse the entire data set, you can download it via the Movebank link.


Reading in the Red Kite Data

rotmilan <- sf::st_read("data/rotmilan.gpkg", quiet = TRUE)



Exercise 1: Kernel Density Estimation
To calculate a 2D kernel density over the red kite data, we use the function density from the R package spatstat. The point pattern is converted to class ppp using as.ppp. We then compare four automatic bandwidth selectors: bw.diggle, bw.CvL, bw.scott, and bw.ppl.


Solution 1:
When running a gaussian Kernel, sigma is the standart deviation of said cernel. Like discussed in the lession, it is often rater challenging and subjective to choose a reasonable kernel size. One usually wants to pick a cernel size that is not to large and blurrs everything into one blob, but also not to small, so that individual occurances allready cause large spikes in the density reading. I am just simply trying out a view sigma values, and then I will pick one value that I believe fits these criterions the best.

rotmilan_ppp <- as.ppp(rotmilan)
swiss_vect   <- terra::vect(swiss_polygon)

sigma_values <- c(500, 2000, 8000, 30000)

sigma_rasters <- lapply(sigma_values, function(s) {
  density(rotmilan_ppp, sigma = s, kernel = "gaussian", eps = 1000) %>%
    terra::rast() %>%
    terra::crop(swiss_vect) %>%
    terra::mask(swiss_vect)
})

tmap_mode("plot")

sigma_maps <- lapply(seq_along(sigma_values), function(i) {
  tm_shape(swiss_polygon) +
    tm_borders(col = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  tm_shape(sigma_rasters[[i]]) +
    tm_raster(
      col = names(sigma_rasters[[i]]),
      col.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
      col.legend = tm_legend(show = FALSE)
    ) +
    tm_title(text = paste0("sigma = ", sigma_values[i], " m"))
})

tmap_arrange(maps = sigma_maps, ncol = 2, nrow = 2)








As mentioned above, picking sigmas arbitrairly is not ideal, as it allows for some subjectivity. Instead certain methods can be used to derive sigma values based on the actuall point pattern present in the data. These methods work differently but they all have in common that they fitt sigma in a way, that optimises some loss function with respect to the data (bw.scott is the odd one out here. Its just a rule of thumb).

bw_methods <- list(
  "bw.diggle" = bw.diggle,
  "bw.CvL"    = bw.CvL,
  "bw.scott"  = function(x) bw.scott(x, isotropic = TRUE),
  "bw.ppl"    = bw.ppl
)

kde_rasters <- lapply(names(bw_methods), function(name) {
  sigma_val <- bw_methods[[name]](rotmilan_ppp)
  cat(name, ": sigma =", round(as.numeric(sigma_val), 1), "m\n")
  density(rotmilan_ppp, sigma = sigma_val, kernel = "gaussian", eps = 1000) %>%
    terra::rast() %>%
    terra::crop(swiss_vect) %>%
    terra::mask(swiss_vect)
})

bw.diggle : sigma = 25.2 m


bw.CvL : sigma = 38038.7 m


bw.scott : sigma = 2805.6 m


bw.ppl : sigma = 944.5 m

names(kde_rasters) <- names(bw_methods)


tmap_mode("plot")

ℹ tmap modes "plot" - "view"

kde_maps <- lapply(names(kde_rasters), function(name) {
  tm_shape(swiss_polygon) +
    tm_borders(col = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  tm_shape(kde_rasters[[name]]) +
    tm_raster(
      col = names(kde_rasters[[name]]),
      col.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
      col.legend = tm_legend(show = FALSE)
    ) +
    tm_title(text = name)
})

tmap_arrange(maps = kde_maps, ncol = 2, nrow = 2)










Interpretation


Exercise 2: Voronoi / Thiessen Polygons (Density)
Thiessen polygons offer an alternative for visualising differences in the density distribution of point data. Smaller polygons indicate areas where observations are denser.

voronoi_density <- rotmilan %>%
    sf::st_geometry() %>%
    sf::st_union() %>%
    sf::st_voronoi() %>%
    sf::st_collection_extract("POLYGON") %>%
    sf::st_as_sf() %>%
    sf::st_intersection(swiss_polygon)

tmap_mode("view")

tm_shape(swiss_polygon) +
    tm_borders(col = "black", lwd = 1) +
tm_shape(voronoi_density) +
    tm_polygons(fill = "lightblue", col = "grey40", lwd = 0.3) +
    tm_title("Red Kite GPS Observations: Voronoi Density")







Interpretation
TODO: Interpret the Voronoi density visualisation. Where are the densest clusters of red kite observations? How does this compare to the KDE results?
